parts.context: A conversation between a customer and a travel agent at a travel agency.
parts.audio_script:
Agent: Good morning, Wanderlust Travel, this is Sarah speaking. How can I help you today?
Customer: Oh, hi. Um, yes, I was hoping to book a train ticket to Manchester, please.
Agent: Certainly. When would you like to travel?
Customer: Uh, let me think... I think Tuesday would be best. Yes, Tuesday.
Agent: Tuesday, right. And would you prefer morning or afternoon?
Customer: Morning, if possible. I have a meeting at noon, so I need to get there before then.
Agent: Okay, let me check the schedule for you. There’s a train at 7:45 am and another at 9:15 am. Which would you prefer?
Customer: Hmm, the 7:45 is a bit early, but I suppose it’s safer. I’ll take that one.
Agent: Great. And will you be travelling alone?
Customer: Yes, just me.
Agent: Alright. Would you like a return ticket or just one way?
Customer: Oh, I’ll need to come back the same day, so a return ticket, please.
Agent: Noted. And do you have any railcards or discounts?
Customer: No, I don’t, unfortunately.
Agent: No problem. So, that’s one adult return ticket to Manchester, departing Tuesday at 7:45 am. Is that correct?
Customer: Yes, that’s right.
Agent: May I have your full name, please?
Customer: Sure, it’s Emily Carter. That’s E-M-I-L-Y, C-A-R-T-E-R.
Agent: Thank you, Emily. And could I have a contact number?
Customer: Yes, it’s 07983 456 210.
Agent: Got it. And how would you like to pay?
Customer: By credit card, please.
Agent: Okay, I’ll process that for you. Oh, before I do, do you have any special requirements? For example, assistance with luggage or a seat preference?
Customer: Um, actually, could I have a window seat if possible?
Agent: Of course. I’ll request a window seat for you. Anything else?
Customer: No, that’s all, thank you.
Agent: Alright, your ticket will be ready for collection at the station. Please bring your card and a form of ID. Is there anything else I can help you with today?
Customer: No, that’s everything. Thanks very much for your help.
Agent: You’re welcome, Emily. Have a great trip to Manchester!
Customer: Thank you. Goodbye.
Agent: Goodbye.

question_sets.instruction: Complete the notes below. Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.

questions.question_text: Customer wants to travel on ______.
Customer prefers to travel in the ______.
The train departs at ______ am.
Customer’s surname is ______.
Customer requests a ______ seat.
Tickets will be collected at the ______.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.question_text: Why does the customer choose the earlier train?
options: B. It arrives before her meeting

questions.question_text: How will the customer pay for the ticket?
What does the customer need to bring to collect the ticket?
Does the customer have any discounts?

parts.context: A monologue by a museum guide about the new City History Museum.
parts.audio_script:
Guide: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to the City History Museum. My name’s Tom, and I’ll be giving you a brief introduction before you begin your visit. Now, the museum opened just last year, after nearly a decade of planning and construction. We’re very proud of what we’ve achieved here, and I hope you’ll enjoy exploring the exhibits. 

Let me start by telling you a little about the building itself. The museum is housed in what used to be the old city library, which was built in 1892. You’ll notice the original stonework and stained glass windows have been carefully restored. We’ve tried to keep as much of the original character as possible, while also making the building accessible to everyone. There are lifts to all floors, and ramps at every entrance, so visitors with mobility needs should have no trouble getting around.

The museum is divided into three main sections. On the ground floor, you’ll find the ‘Origins’ gallery, which covers the early history of the city, from Roman times up to the Industrial Revolution. There are some fascinating artefacts here, including a Roman coin hoard discovered just a few years ago. Upstairs, on the first floor, is the ‘Growth and Change’ gallery. This section looks at how the city developed during the 19th and 20th centuries, with displays on local industries, transport, and famous residents. Finally, on the top floor, we have the ‘Modern City’ gallery, which explores life in the city today, with interactive exhibits and a special area for children.

If you’re interested in guided tours, these run every hour on the hour, starting from the main entrance. Tours last about 45 minutes and are included in your ticket price. For those who prefer to explore at their own pace, we have audio guides available in several languages. Just ask at the information desk if you’d like to borrow one.

Before you begin your visit, a quick note about facilities. Toilets are located on every floor, and there’s a café on the ground floor serving drinks, snacks, and light lunches. The gift shop, which is just next to the exit, has a range of souvenirs, books, and locally made crafts. Please remember that food and drink are not allowed in the galleries, but you’re welcome to use the café or picnic area outside.

We hope you enjoy your time at the City History Museum. If you have any questions, please don’t hesitate to ask any member of staff. Thank you, and have a great visit.

question_sets.instruction: Match each gallery with the correct description. Write the correct letter, A, B or C, next to questions 11-15.

questions.question_text: Origins gallery
Growth and Change gallery
Modern City gallery

parts.context: A conversation between two university students, Anna and Mark, and their tutor, Dr. Patel, about a group project.
parts.audio_script:
Dr. Patel: Good morning, Anna, Mark. Thanks for coming in. I wanted to check how your group project is progressing. 
Anna: Morning, Dr. Patel. Um, yes, we’ve made a start, but we’ve had a few issues, actually.
Mark: Yeah, we’re a bit behind schedule, to be honest. We had some trouble dividing up the tasks.
Dr. Patel: I see. Can you tell me more about that?
Anna: Well, at first, we tried to split the research evenly, but it turned out that some sections were much more complicated than others. For example, the literature review took a lot longer than we expected.
Mark: And, uh, I think we underestimated how much time the data analysis would take. We only started that last week.
Dr. Patel: That’s quite common. Have you managed to meet regularly as a group?
Anna: Not really. We all have different timetables, so it’s been hard to find a time that suits everyone.
Mark: We’ve mostly been communicating by email, but it’s not the same as meeting in person.
Dr. Patel: No, it isn’t. I’d recommend setting up a regular meeting, even if it’s just once a week. It helps keep everyone on track.
Anna: That’s a good idea. We’ll try to do that.
Dr. Patel: How about the presentation? Have you decided who will present which parts?
Mark: Not yet. We thought we’d wait until we’d finished the research.
Anna: I’m a bit nervous about presenting, to be honest. I haven’t done much public speaking before.
Dr. Patel: That’s understandable. Maybe you could practise together as a group. It often helps to rehearse in front of others.
Mark: Yeah, that makes sense. We could do a trial run next week.
Dr. Patel: Excellent. And remember, you can use the seminar rooms if you need a space to practise.
Anna: Thank you. That’s helpful.
Dr. Patel: One more thing—have you thought about how you’ll reference your sources?
Mark: We’re planning to use the Harvard system, like you suggested in the lecture.
Dr. Patel: Good. Make sure everyone in the group understands how to use it correctly. Incorrect referencing is a common reason for losing marks.
Anna: We’ll double-check that.
Dr. Patel: Great. Well, it sounds like you’re making progress, even if there have been a few bumps along the way. If you need any more help, just let me know.
Mark: Thanks, Dr. Patel.
Anna: Yes, thank you.
Dr. Patel: You’re welcome. Good luck with the rest of the project.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.question_text: What problem did the group face with dividing tasks?
options: A. Some tasks were more difficult than others
B. No one wanted to do the research
C. They had too many people

options: B. Data analysis took longer than expected

parts.context: A university lecturer giving a talk on the importance of sleep for students.
parts.audio_script:
Lecturer: Good morning, everyone. Today, I’d like to talk to you about a topic that’s often overlooked but absolutely vital for your academic success and overall well-being: sleep. Now, I know many of you are probably thinking, ‘I don’t have time to sleep!’ or ‘I’ll catch up on the weekend.’ But research shows that sleep is not something you can simply make up for later. 

Let’s start with why sleep is so important. During sleep, your brain processes information from the day, consolidates memories, and clears out toxins that build up while you’re awake. Without enough sleep, your ability to concentrate, remember information, and solve problems is significantly reduced. In fact, studies have shown that students who regularly get less than seven hours of sleep a night perform worse academically than those who get enough rest.

So, what stops students from getting enough sleep? Well, there are several factors. First, there’s the pressure to meet deadlines, which often leads to late-night studying. Then there’s the use of electronic devices—phones, laptops, tablets—late at night. The blue light from these screens can interfere with your body’s production of melatonin, the hormone that helps you fall asleep. Social activities and part-time jobs can also cut into your sleep time. And let’s not forget stress, which can make it hard to relax and fall asleep, even when you’re tired.

Now, you might be wondering, ‘How much sleep do I actually need?’ Most adults need between seven and nine hours per night. Teenagers and young adults, which includes most university students, are at the higher end of that range. But it’s not just about quantity—quality matters too. Interrupted or restless sleep can be just as harmful as not getting enough.

So, what can you do to improve your sleep? First, try to keep a regular sleep schedule, even on weekends. Going to bed and waking up at the same time each day helps regulate your body clock. Second, create a relaxing bedtime routine—maybe read a book, listen to calming music, or do some gentle stretching. Avoid caffeine and heavy meals late in the evening, and try to limit screen time for at least an hour before bed. If you find yourself lying awake, don’t just stare at the ceiling—get up, do something quiet for a few minutes, then try again.

Finally, if you’re consistently having trouble sleeping, it’s important to seek help. The university has counselling services and health professionals who can offer advice and support. Remember, sleep isn’t a luxury—it’s a necessity. Prioritising sleep will help you stay healthy, focused, and ready to succeed. Thank you.

question_sets.instruction: Complete the sentences below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS for each answer.

questions.question_text: Sleep helps the brain to consolidate ______.
Students who sleep less than seven hours perform worse ______.

Interrupted sleep can be as harmful as insufficient ______.

What should you do if you can’t sleep?

passages.title: The Evolution of Urban Green Spaces
passages.content:
Urban green spaces, encompassing parks, gardens, and natural reserves within city boundaries, have undergone significant transformation since the onset of industrialization. In the nineteenth century, rapid urbanization led to the proliferation of densely populated cities, often characterized by poor air quality and limited access to natural environments. Early urban planners, influenced by the public health movement, advocated for the creation of parks as a means to counteract the deleterious effects of industrial life. Notably, Frederick Law Olmsted, the designer of New York’s Central Park, argued that green spaces were essential for both physical and psychological well-being. His vision was echoed in cities across Europe and North America, where municipal authorities began to allocate land for public parks, botanical gardens, and tree-lined boulevards.

The twentieth century witnessed a diversification in the functions and forms of urban green spaces. No longer confined to ornamental or recreational purposes, these areas increasingly served ecological roles, such as supporting biodiversity, mitigating urban heat islands, and managing stormwater runoff. The emergence of the environmental movement in the 1960s and 1970s further elevated the status of urban nature, prompting city governments to integrate green infrastructure into urban planning. Initiatives such as green belts, urban forests, and community gardens proliferated, reflecting a growing recognition of the multifaceted benefits provided by urban vegetation.

Recent decades have seen a paradigm shift towards the concept of sustainable cities, wherein green spaces are regarded as integral components of urban ecosystems. Contemporary research underscores the importance of accessible green areas for promoting social cohesion, enhancing mental health, and fostering environmental stewardship among urban residents. Moreover, the advent of climate change has intensified efforts to expand and connect green spaces, with the aim of increasing urban resilience to extreme weather events. Cities such as Singapore and Copenhagen have pioneered innovative approaches, including vertical gardens, green roofs, and interconnected park networks, to maximize the ecological and social value of limited urban land.

Despite these advances, challenges persist in ensuring equitable access to green spaces. Socioeconomic disparities often dictate the distribution and quality of urban nature, with marginalized communities frequently experiencing a deficit of greenery. Urban densification and competing land uses further complicate efforts to preserve and expand green areas. Policymakers and planners are thus confronted with the task of balancing development pressures with the imperative to maintain and enhance urban green infrastructure.

In conclusion, the evolution of urban green spaces reflects broader shifts in societal values, scientific understanding, and policy priorities. From their origins as sanctuaries from industrial blight to their current role as vital elements of sustainable urbanism, green spaces continue to shape—and be shaped by—the cities in which they are embedded.

question_sets.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information given in the passage? For questions 1–7, write TRUE if the statement agrees with the information, FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

passages.title: The Ethics and Economics of Artificial Intelligence in the Workplace
passages.content:
The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into the workplace has generated considerable debate among economists, ethicists, and policymakers. Proponents argue that AI-driven automation enhances productivity, reduces operational costs, and enables the reallocation of human labor to more creative or complex tasks. For instance, in manufacturing, AI-powered robots can perform repetitive or hazardous tasks with greater efficiency and precision than their human counterparts. Similarly, in the service sector, algorithms are increasingly used to optimize logistics, personalize customer experiences, and streamline administrative processes.

However, the economic benefits of AI are accompanied by significant ethical and social challenges. One major concern is the potential for widespread job displacement, particularly among low- and middle-skilled workers. While some analysts contend that technological innovation historically creates more jobs than it destroys, others caution that the rapid pace and scale of AI adoption may outstrip the capacity of labor markets to adapt. This could exacerbate income inequality and social stratification, especially in regions with limited access to education and retraining opportunities.

Another ethical dilemma pertains to the transparency and accountability of AI systems. Many algorithms operate as 'black boxes,' making decisions based on complex, often opaque, data-driven processes. This lack of transparency raises questions about fairness, bias, and the potential for discrimination, particularly in sensitive domains such as hiring, lending, and law enforcement. Ensuring that AI systems are explainable and subject to oversight is therefore a priority for regulators and industry leaders alike.

The deployment of AI also has implications for workplace surveillance and privacy. Advanced monitoring tools can track employee performance, monitor communications, and even predict behavioral trends. While such technologies may improve efficiency and security, they also risk infringing on individual autonomy and eroding trust between employers and employees. Balancing the legitimate interests of organizations with the rights of workers remains a contentious issue.

In response to these challenges, various stakeholders have proposed frameworks for the ethical governance of AI in the workplace. These include principles such as transparency, accountability, inclusivity, and respect for human dignity. Some governments have introduced legislation mandating algorithmic audits, impact assessments, and the right to explanation for individuals affected by automated decisions. Meanwhile, industry associations have developed voluntary codes of conduct and best practice guidelines to promote responsible AI deployment.

Ultimately, the future trajectory of AI in the workplace will depend on the interplay between technological innovation, regulatory intervention, and societal values. While AI holds the promise of transforming work for the better, realizing its benefits while mitigating its risks will require ongoing dialogue, collaboration, and vigilance from all sectors of society.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

passages.title: The Paradox of Memory: Neuroscience, Culture, and the Construction of the Past
passages.content:
Memory, once conceived as a passive repository of experiences, is now understood as a dynamic and reconstructive process shaped by both biological mechanisms and cultural contexts. Neuroscientific research has revealed that the act of remembering is not a simple retrieval of stored information but rather an active reconstruction, involving the reassembly of disparate neural traces. This process is susceptible to distortion, as memories are influenced by subsequent experiences, emotions, and even the act of recollection itself. The malleability of memory has profound implications for individual identity, collective history, and the reliability of eyewitness testimony.

At the biological level, memory formation and retrieval are mediated by complex interactions among various brain regions, notably the hippocampus, prefrontal cortex, and amygdala. The consolidation of memories involves the strengthening of synaptic connections, a process modulated by neurochemical signals and subject to disruption by stress, trauma, or neurodegenerative disease. Recent studies employing functional neuroimaging have demonstrated that recalling a memory activates many of the same neural circuits engaged during the original experience, yet the pattern of activation is never identical. This neural plasticity underlies both the adaptability and the fallibility of human memory.

Cultural factors further complicate the landscape of memory. Anthropologists and psychologists have documented significant cross-cultural variation in the ways individuals encode, organize, and narrate their past. In some societies, memory is highly individualized, emphasizing personal achievement and autonomy, while in others, it is embedded within collective narratives that prioritize group identity and continuity. Rituals, commemorations, and oral traditions serve as vehicles for the transmission and reinforcement of shared memories, shaping not only what is remembered but also how it is interpreted.

The intersection of neuroscience and culture is particularly evident in the phenomenon of collective memory. Historical events—wars, revolutions, or natural disasters—are remembered not merely as aggregates of individual recollections but as socially constructed narratives, subject to revision and contestation. The selective emphasis on certain events, the omission of others, and the reinterpretation of meanings reflect the interests and values of particular groups. This dynamic process can foster social cohesion but also perpetuate conflict, as competing memories vie for legitimacy in the public sphere.

The paradox of memory lies in its dual capacity to preserve and to distort. While memory enables individuals and societies to maintain continuity with the past, it is inherently selective and reconstructive, vulnerable to error and manipulation. Advances in neuroscience have illuminated the mechanisms underlying these processes, yet they also raise ethical questions about the potential for technological interventions—such as memory enhancement or erasure—to alter the fabric of personal and collective identity.

In sum, memory is not a static archive but a living, evolving phenomenon at the nexus of biology and culture. Understanding its complexities requires an interdisciplinary approach, integrating insights from neuroscience, psychology, anthropology, and history. As societies grapple with the challenges of historical reconciliation, trauma, and the preservation of cultural heritage, the study of memory remains both a scientific and a moral imperative.

tasks.instruction: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
tasks.data_description: The table below shows the percentage of households in five different countries (Canada, Germany, Japan, Brazil, and South Africa) that owned various types of electronic devices in the years 2000 and 2020. The devices listed are televisions, computers, mobile phones, and washing machines. In 2000, Canada had the highest percentage of households with televisions (95%), followed by Germany (92%), Japan (90%), Brazil (75%), and South Africa (60%). By 2020, television ownership increased slightly in all countries, with Canada and Germany both reaching 98%, Japan at 97%, Brazil at 85%, and South Africa at 75%. Computer ownership in 2000 was highest in Germany (60%) and Canada (55%), while Japan had 50%, Brazil 20%, and South Africa 10%. By 2020, computer ownership rose significantly, with Germany at 90%, Canada at 88%, Japan at 85%, Brazil at 60%, and South Africa at 40%. Mobile phone ownership saw the most dramatic increase: in 2000, only 30% of Canadian households, 25% in Germany, 20% in Japan, 10% in Brazil, and 5% in South Africa owned mobile phones. By 2020, these figures soared to 95% in Canada, 93% in Germany, 90% in Japan, 80% in Brazil, and 70% in South Africa. Washing machine ownership also increased, but less dramatically: in 2000, Canada had 80%, Germany 75%, Japan 70%, Brazil 40%, and South Africa 30%. By 2020, these numbers rose to 90% in Canada, 88% in Germany, 85% in Japan, 60% in Brazil, and 50% in South Africa.

tasks.instruction: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Write about the following topic. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

part_3.topic: The Importance of Creativity in Daily Life
questions: Do you think creativity is important in everyday life? Why or why not?
In what ways can schools and workplaces encourage people to be more creative?
